coredata.dc:title: Revolutionizing nanozymes: The synthesis, enzyme-mimicking capabilities of carbon dots, and advancements in catalytic mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Chun-Mei
Xiao, Xiao-Shan
Chen, Jing-Yi
He, Wen-Yun
Wang, Si-Si
Qin, Yuan
He, Shao-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes, a revolutionary category of engineered artificial enzymes based on nanomaterials, have been developed to overcome the inherent limitations of natural enzymes, such as the high cost associated with storage and their fragility. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as compelling candidates for various applications due to their versatile properties. Particularly noteworthy are CDs with a range of surface functional groups that exhibit enzyme-like behavior, combining exceptional performance with catalytic capabilities. This review explores the methodologies used for synthesizing CDs with enzyme mimicking capabilities, highlighting potential avenues such as doping and hybrid nanozymes to enhance their catalytic efficacy. Moreover, a comprehensive overview of CDs that mimick the activities of various oxidoreductases-like peroxidase, catalase, oxidase/laccase, and superoxide dismutase-like is provided. The focus is on the in-depth exploration of the mechanisms, advancements and practical applications of each oxidoreductase-like function exhibited by CD nanozymes. Drawing upon these exhaustive summaries and analyses, the review identifies the prevailing challenges that hinder the seamless integration of CDs into real-world applications and offers forward-looking perspectives for future directions.

Nanozymes

Biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes, a revolutionary category of engineered artificial enzymes based on nanomaterials, have been developed to overcome the inherent limitations of natural enzymes, such as the high cost associated with storage and their fragility. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as compelling candidates for various applications due to their versatile properties. Particularly noteworthy are CDs with a range of surface functional groups that exhibit enzyme-like behavior, combining exceptional performance with catalytic capabilities. This review explores the methodologies used for synthesizing CDs with enzyme mimicking capabilities, highlighting potential avenues such as doping and hybrid nanozymes to enhance their catalytic efficacy. Moreover, a comprehensive overview of CDs that mimick the activities of various oxidoreductases-like peroxidase, catalase, oxidase/laccase, and superoxide dismutase-like is provided. The focus is on the in-depth exploration of the mechanisms, advancements and practical applications of each oxidoreductase-like function exhibited by CD nanozymes. Drawing upon these exhaustive summaries and analyses, the review identifies the prevailing challenges that hinder the seamless integration of CDs into real-world applications and offers forward-looking perspectives for future directions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes are organic catalysts synthesized within biological organisms, predominantly in the form of proteins, with RNA also playing a minor role in their composition [1,2]. As catalysts of extraordinary power in the biological world, enzymes are characterized by their extraordinary ability to accelerate a wide range of chemical reactions. The process is executed with exceptional efficiency, maintaining stringent substrate specificity and selectivity, and yielding consistently high outputs. This achievement is notable for being realized under conditions of remarkable gentleness, underscoring the sophisticated and nuanced manner in which these enzymes function [3–5].Given their unparalleled catalytic efficacy, enzymes are extensively utilized in both the medical and industrial spheres. Nevertheless, several challenges impede the widespread practical use of natural enzymes. These include their susceptibility to denaturation and degradation under severe conditions. Furthermore, the retrieval and recycling of enzyme are frequently challenging. Moreover, the expenses related to their preparation and purification processes are particularly high, which further constrains their application across diverse scenarios [6,7]. For example, in terms of environmental sensitivity, enzymes are at risk of structural damage under extreme pH conditions, which can affect both their primary and tertiary configurations. Consequently, this damage can lead to the denaturation of the enzyme's constituent subunits, culminating in the loss of its catalytic potency [8,9]. The issue is further intensified due to the fact that certain samples may have pH values that are significantly different from neutral [10]. Furthermore, the inherent challenges associated with the preparation and purification of naturally occurring enzymes contribute to their elevated final costs. This factor, in turn, restricts their broader application. Currently, the advancement of synthetic biomimetic materials has emerged as a highly compelling area of focus, yielding a plethora of noteworthy advancements [11,12]. To overcome these challenges, scientists are working hard to develop artificial enzyme mimics [13,14]. Thanks to the explosion of nanotechnology, biotechnology and computational theory, the use of nanomaterials to replace enzymes is becoming increasingly popular [15,16]. Consequently, there has been a surge in the pursuit of synthetic enzymes that can provide enhanced stability and reduced costs compared to natural enzymes, positioning these artificial alternatives as feasible substitutes for conventional enzymatic solutions.

Nanozyme are nanomaterials between 1 and 100 nm in size with enzyme-like properties [17,18], have been a subject of study since the 1990s, with the term “nanozyme” being introduced by Scrimin et al. in 2004. Ever since Yan and colleagues uncovered in 2007 that ferromagnetic nanoparticles possess the capacity to emulate enzymatic activities [19], we have witnessed a revolutionary change in the paradigm of nanozyme systems. The field of small-scale science and its applications has undergone significant development, driven by rapid advances and a broadening understanding of its concepts [20]. During this evolutionary journey, a range of materials - from metal-based to metal-free compounds and their composites - have been used to produce an impressive array of nanozymes [4,21]. As synthetic catalysts, nanozymes present numerous advantages over naturally occurring enzymes counterparts, including improved stability, reduced cost and increased resilience to environmental factors. Their ease of synthesis, extended shelf-life and tunable activity - tailored by size and composition - make nanozymes promising candidates for substitution across a range of scientific disciplines. These characteristics arise from their intrinsic properties, establishing nanozymes as groundbreaking alternatives with significant potential across various scientific domains. Nanozymes have introduced pioneering opportunities that surpass the intrinsic limitations of natural enzymes. These limitations encompass their propensity for denaturation under severe pH and thermal conditions, constrained reusability and recyclability, and elevated production expenses. Moreover, the catalytic efficacy of nanozymes can be precisely tailored through a deliberate and meticulously crafted synthesis process. Considering these advantages, an extensive array of synthetic compounds and materials, engineered to emulate enzymatic activities, has been devised and applied across diverse industries. These include biosensing, bioimaging, antibacterial applications, antioxidant strategies, therapeutic interventions, and environmental conservation efforts, among others [22–24]. Thus far, an expansive repertoire of nanomaterials has garnered recognition for their capacity to emulate enzymatic functions, as evidenced by their catalytic actions. These include nanoparticles such as iron oxide, Prussian blue, vanadium oxide and cerium oxide, as well as noble metal nanoparticles. Carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) have garnered attention for their ability to emulate enzymatic functions, showcasing potential as catalysts in various chemical reactions akin to those facilitated by natural enzymes [8,25–29].

Among these, CNMs stand out due to their remarkable attributes, making them a focal point in materials science. Particularly noteworthy are CNMs such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [30], graphene [29], and CDs [31], which are celebrated for their remarkable stability, economic viability, and the ease with which they can be synthesized and modified. These characteristics have not only established CNMs as a prominent family within materials science but also underscore their potential as a new class of nanozymes. The catalytic performance of CNMs and their hybrid configurations has drawn substantial interest, with a multitude of investigations highlighting their versatility for diverse applications. These include CDs, which are amorphous carbon nanoparticles between 2 and 10 nm in size [32]. Boasting a diverse array of functional groups on their surfaces, these materials exhibit remarkable quantum yield and biological functionality, epitomizing the pinnacle of innovative breakthroughs. Interestingly, CDs hold great promise in biocatalysis due to their ability to replicate a variety of enzyme catalytic sites and exhibit diverse oxidoreductase functions such as peroxidase, laccase, catalase, and SOD [33]. In addition, various strategies have been deployed to enhance the enzymatic mimicry of CDs. These methods involve integrating diverse dopants and crafting hybrid nanozymes to capitalize on their synergistic interactions [34]. Such progress has significantly broadened the versatility of CDs in biomedical applications.

Regarding the biomedical applications of CDs nanozymes, several pivotal factors merit attention: (1) the inherent catalytic attributes of these nanozymes are of paramount importance; (2) a deliberate and rational design of the nanozyme architectures is indispensable; (3) their biological effects and compatibility with living organisms necessitate rigorous assessment [35]. The purpose of this review is to outline their synthesis, enzyme mimicking capabilities and advances in catalytic mechanisms. Here we look at CDs nanozymes that mimic key enzymes such as peroxidase, oxidase/laccase, catalase and superoxide dismutase. We highlight the importance of their precursors, the techniques used to synthesize them, and their various uses and applications. We are also exploring the manufacture of doped CDs and the innovative development of hybrid systems that exploit synergistic effects to enhance performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classification and synthesis approaches of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The arc discharge phenomenon takes place in a low-pressure chamber surrounded by an inert gas. Anodes are typically made from powdered carbon precursors, while cathodes are predominantly made from pure graphite. As the system functions, the resources undergo vaporization and utilization, culminating in their sublimation and migration towards the cathode, where they give rise to nanostructures. In their pioneering investigation, Xu et al. achieved a remarkable leap forward in the domain of nanomaterial exploration through the refinement of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNT) extracted from arc discharge residue [44]. Thorough characterization was conducted on the materials, which consisted of short tubular carbon and luminescent nanoparticles originating from SWNT. The material's hydrophilicity was enhanced by the deliberate incorporation of carboxyl functional groups into the arc-treated residue via an oxidation process using HNO₃ solution. The isolated precipitate was recovered by treatment with a NaOH solution and subsequently fractionated via gel electrophoresis, unveiling the distinct configurations of luminescent carbon and short tubular carbon. These formations were recognized as advanced manifestations of carbon materials and CDs. The arc discharge technique for producing CDs, while yielding robustly fluorescent particles, is not exempt from the challenges of inconsistent particle size distribution and diminished production output, constraints that impede their suitability for large-scale applications. The synthesis from a complex matrix introduces a significant impurity load, complicating the purification and extraction processes and leading to a subdued fluorescence quantum yield. Yet, the CDs' minuscule dimensions and potent fluorescence, unaltered by surface modifications, remain a notable benefit in their fabrication. Further inquiries have revealed that the spacing between electrodes significantly impacts the diameter and quality of the synthesized CDs [40]. Optimizing the alignment of magnetic field lines can improve the movement and production process of the arc. The presence of a magnetic field can boost the deposition growth rate and enhance the purity of the final CDs, providing a promising method to enhance their purity from discharge.

Laser ablation heats a substance, forming a plasma and ionising it. Sun's team created a carbon matrix by fusing graphite with cement and then curing it in argon. The resulting CDs, which have optical properties similar to semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) but with lower toxicity, were passivated with organic substances [43]. In a significant study, researchers conducted a pivotal experiment employing a short-pulsed infrared laser to vaporize non-crystalline carbon [67]. The research disclosed a clear spatial distribution of nanodiamonds close to the laser focus in region 1, contrasting with the spontaneous appearance of CDs both in the ablation zone (region 2) and in the extended plasma region (region 3). The complex interplay of the laser ablation regions resulted in a spectrum of reaction products that posed a challenge to the uniformity of the product synthesis (Fig. 2A and B). Using nanocarbon substrates and basic solvents as liquid matrices for CDs fabrication [68], CNMs were carefully distributed into a solvent. After meticulous ultrasonic treatment, a carefully measured 4 mL of the suspension was irradiated in a glass chamber with a 523 nm laser in a controlled manner, culminating in the production of CDs from the supernatant (Fig. 2C). The synthesis approach employed in this meticulous study is distinguished by its remarkable simplicity, unparalleled convenience, and unmatched efficacy relative to prior fabrication methods, with the distinct advantage of an unfocused laser beam.

By exposing a graphite sheet to laser irradiation within a polymeric solution, Hu and colleagues adeptly synthesized CDs with a controlled size distribution ranging from 3 to 13 nm [45]. In their study, they found that manipulating the laser pulse parameters could significantly affect the nucleation and growth dynamics within the bubble, thereby yielding a diverse array of particle size distributions. This finding implies that employing a laser with an extended pulse duration may enhance the precision for controlling the dimensions of a broad spectrum of nanoscale structures. CDs have been successfully synthesized through the innovative method of laser ablation, utilizing an affordable carbon textile. This process employs a dual-beam pulsed laser, which operates at high velocities, ensuring not only remarkable efficiency but also a high degree of precision [69]. By employing a beam splitter to divide a single laser beam into a dual-beam configuration, the ablation process was significantly expedited. This innovative approach not only enhanced the uniformity of the synthesized CDs but also achieved an impressive photoluminescence QY of up to 35.4 % (Fig. 2D). The synthesis of DMSO-CQDs through two-beam pulsed laser ablation (PLA) is a sophisticated process that involves directing laser pulses from two orthogonal directions onto a carbon fabric. This method triggers a Coulomb explosion, leading to the formation of a plasma plume. The plume is characterized by extremely high temperatures, pressures, and localized electromagnetic fields, which are crucial for the formation of CDs with desired properties (Fig. 2E). Laser ablation, despite its precision in manipulating nanoscale structures across a wide range, faces significant drawbacks such as the high cost of equipment and consequent limited adoption. These factors constrain its broader application in achieving uniform product synthesis on a large scale. Despite these challenges, such technique continues to be a subject of interest due to its ability to produce CDs with unique properties.

Oxidation is a method for synthesizing CDs by breaking down large carbonaceous precursors into smaller carbon nanomaterials using potent oxidizing agents like H2SO4 and HNO3. Considering the role of the oxidizing agent, the methods can be categorized into those employing chemical oxidants and those utilizing electrochemical oxidation. The oxidizing agent is essential for breaking the chemical bonds in the carbon-rich material, thereby facilitating the production of distinct carbon nanoparticles. The progress of the reaction depends significantly on the oxidizing power of the agent used. Common chemical oxidizers include sulfuric acid and nitric acid. One study synthesized CDs using sulfuric acid to dehydrate a carbohydrate precursor in an aqueous solution, followed by decomposition with nitric acid to produce discrete carbon nanoparticles [70]. After functionalization with amine compounds, visible photoluminescence was observed (Fig. 3A). This demonstrated that nitric acid treatment not only breaks down carbonaceous materials but also alters their elemental composition by increasing oxygen content. Similarly, three activated carbon sources were effectively converted into photoluminescent CDs using nitric acid treatment [71]. The non-crystalline form of the activated carbon was transformed into individual CDs, which were subsequently functionalized to enhance dispersibility and biocompatibility (Fig. 3B). The CDs derived from diverse activated carbon sources displayed uniform size and exceptional dispersion, suggesting minimal impact of the carbon origin on the ultimate product characteristics. In general, the dimensions, configuration, and attributes of CDs produced through chemical oxidation are predominantly contingent upon the specific oxidizing agent and the conditions of oxidation utilized. Starch, a low-cost carbon resource, is used to produce water-soluble CDs with excellent fluorescent properties through chemical oxidation. Subsequently, the resulting product is dispersed in a blend of oxidizing agents that includes deionized water, acetic acid (HAc), and H2O2. This mixture undergoes ultrasonic treatment and reflux before being filtered. The filtrate is then neutralized with sodium hydroxide (NaOH), concentrated, and dialyzed by immersing it in a membrane tubing. The subsequent solution is reduced in volume, culminating in the formation of a pale-yellow CDs powder upon drying under vacuum conditions, as depicted in Fig. 3C. The oxidizing agent not only facilitates the removal of the carbon precursor, but also introduces hydroxyl and carboxyl functionalities into the CDs. This methodology is versatile across a broad spectrum of carbon sources and does not necessitate intricate or costly equipment [72]. Consequently, the overuse of oxidants leads to the need for partial neutralizing agents, which in turn boosts the quantity of reaction media necessary. Environmental concerns are also raised by the use of concentrated acids and alkalis.

Electrochemical oxidation employs a strong oxidative potential to strip carbon sources on the electrode surface, forming CDs - a process analogous to arc discharge synthesis. In a pioneering research conducted in 2007, an electrochemical cell was ingeniously designed, featuring a multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) as the active working electrode, which was seamlessly integrated with carbon paper. The cell, with a Pt wire as counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as reference, was operated with an acetonitrile-based electrolyte solution supplemented with 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium perchlorate [40]. By applying a cyclic sweep from -2 V to +2 V, a sequential color transition from transparent to yellow and then to dark brown was detected, indicating the formation of CDs as a result of the TBA cation stripping defects within the nanotube structure [73]. Subsequent work showed that graphite electrodes with basic alcohol electrolytes could also produce CDs through 5 V electrochemical oxidation, with color changes upon exposure to air due to CDs oxidation (Fig. 4B). The CDs enabled sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions and were also suitable for cellular imaging. Moreover, it is noteworthy that small molecules can be converted into CDs through the process of electrochemical synthesis. Du's research team utilized l-ascorbic acid as a precursor for the preparation of CDs via one-step electrochemical oxidation. The experimental setup involved platinum wire electrodes in an l-ascorbic acid solution, leading to CDs of varying sizes over time (Fig. 4A) [46]. The oxidation process in electrolysis converts -OH groups to -COOH groups, leading to an increase in the size and oxidation level of CDs over time. Unlike larger molecules that desorb, smaller molecules tend to aggregate during the formation of CDs, causing a progressive increase in size. Consequently, the size and oxidation level of CDs can be tailored by regulating reaction duration and electrical parameters.

The groundbreaking “Lab on chip” technology has transformed lab practices by miniaturizing standard laboratory operations onto sleek, chip-sized devices. In a recent breakthrough, Xu and their team have crafted an adept electrochemical technique on these chips. This innovative approach enables the straightforward synthesis of CDs directly from screen-printed carbon electrodes. The electrolyte in this cutting-edge process is a state-of-the-art ionic liquid (IL)-based aqueous solution, which functions as the conduit for the electrochemical reactions. The resulting CDs demonstrated remarkable dispersion in water and outstanding luminescent characteristics (Fig. 4C) [74]. The synthesis was accomplished in roughly 30 min, as evidenced by the consistent hue of the solution and the steady state of the electric current. The length of the synthesis and the initial current were influenced by the concentration of the IL in the electrolyte solution. This streamlined reaction setup allowed for swift mass transfer and reduced the consumption of raw materials, enabling easy exploration of different reaction conditions. However, this synthesis method is still in the research phase due to uncertainties in the amplification process, making it unsuitable for mass-scale manufacturing. The electrochemical oxidation process offers several advantages, including ease of operation, substantial product yield, and the ability to control reaction parameters. Nonetheless, it necessitates the use of specialized equipment and is limited by the compatibility of the precursors it can process. This technique, despite its operational ease and high efficiency, is dependent on the accessibility of suitable precursors and the implementation of specific apparatus.

Pyrolysis is an effective technique for synthesizing CDs from organic carbon sources. In this process, the carbonaceous raw materials are gently heated to high temperatures in an inert environment, which catalyzes a smooth and gradual transformation into CDs. This transformation is meticulously realized through a sequence of steps: the initial application of thermal treatment to initiate the process, the subsequent removal of moisture to maintain a dry environment, the strategic breakdown of molecular structures, and the final formation of a carbon-rich structure [75]. In particular, the low-temperature pyrolysis of watermelon rinds enabled the large-scale, simple production of CDs with desirable properties such as small size, strong blue fluorescence and stability under different conditions. Such biomass waste represents a cheap and convenient carbon source for this approach [47]. Key reaction variables such as temperature and time need to be optimized. Inadequate levels can inhibit full carbonization into CDs, while excessive levels can damage the structure and optical properties. Temperature plays a critical role due to the endothermic nature, with over-oxidation or surface degradation at too high levels [76]. Reaction time has a similar effect on carbonization, but the effect depends on the temperature used [77]. Overall, pyrolysis offers a scalable and cost-effective pathway for the synthesis of CDs from readily available, natural precursors, bypassing the need for additional processing steps. However, meticulous parameter tuning is essential to achieve optimal conditions and ensure the production of high-quality fluorescent nanoparticles. Despite the precision pyrolysis can offer in crafting nanostructures, it faces significant challenges, such as the high cost of equipment and limited adoption, which restrict its broader application in uniform product synthesis on a large scale. Yet, the potential of pyrolysis to produce CDs with unique properties continues to draw interest, promising advanced applications in the field of materials science.

It has been shown in studies on sonochemistry that subjecting a liquid to sound waves of high enough amplitude leads to the formation and pulsation of gaseous cavities. During this process, extreme transient conditions are generated locally (temperatures up to 20,000 K and pressures of thousands of bars). This phenomenon provides a unique means of activating chemical transformations and synthesizing compounds not readily accessible using conventional methodologies [78]. Furthermore, ultrasonication aids in the uniform distribution of the reaction's byproducts across the substrate, effectively preventing their clumping. This technique promotes a more homogeneous dispersion, ensuring that the end products are evenly spread, which is crucial for the subsequent steps of the process. In contrast to thermally driven syntheses, ultrasound enables the localized and instantaneous generation of heat required for chemical reactions, which is then rapidly absorbed by the solvent upon completion. As such, there are negligible macroscale temperature variations, obviating the need for specialized high-temperature, high-pressure equipment [79]. Collectively, the ultrasonic technique promotes uniform byproduct distribution on substrates, mitigating aggregation and ensuring product uniformity. It offers a mild-environment alternative to thermal synthesis, eschewing the need for high-temperature, high-pressure apparatus. Yet, the intricacies of the ultrasonic process, with its stochastic and non-uniform nature, complicate large-scale reactor control, resulting in efficiency, repeatability, and energy consumption issues that hinder its scalability in industrial synthesis.

Ball milling provides an environmentally-friendly approach for synthesizing CDs, which have been utilized for the synthesis of carbon dots (CFCD) from spent coffee grounds (SCGs), an easily obtainable carbon source [80]. This mild technique utilizes benign mechanical forces rather than harsh reagents to fracture chemical and particulate bonds. SCGs and ethanol were blended in plastic containers for milling at a speed of 150 rpm under ambient temperature conditions, harnessing kinetic energy to confer new functionalities upon the mixture. The resultant precipitated particles were subsequently extracted from the solution via centrifugation, followed by dialysis against deionized water to achieve purification of the solution. The purified CFCD was then obtained after the drying process. To augment the properties of the CFCD, wet ball milling was employed to introduce co-doping from oxygen and nitrogen-containing small molecules, thereby further tailoring the material's attributes. The resulting CDs, doped with carboxylic acid groups (CA-CFCD) or nitrogen (N-CFCD), exhibited enhanced stability when dispersed in hydrophilic solvents, surpassing that of the unmodified CFCD. In particular, a microgel matrix encapsulated with CA-CFCD showed potential as a practical fluorescent probe for rapid metal ion detection. Within seconds, it confirmed its potential utilization by detecting Fe3+ via fluorescence change. Appropriate precursors are pivotal for the preparation process, necessitating a suitable particle size and crystallinity to enhance exfoliation and functionalization during ball milling, as well as a high carbon content. Despite the high processing capacity and simplicity of ball milling, the selection of suitable raw materials is somewhat constrained. Moreover, this green synthesis technique, leveraging benign mechanical forces, effectively transforms recycled coffee waste into carbon nanostructures, underscoring its environmental and material science significance.

The hydrothermal or solvothermal synthesis is a process in which reactants are subjected to specific temperatures and pressures within a closed vessel, such as an autoclave, in the presence of water, organic solvents, or non-aqueous liquids. This approach takes advantage of the unique conditions to catalyze chemical reactions [81]. Hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis is frequently utilized, recognized for its simplicity, safety, and efficacy. Furthermore, the raw materials utilized in hydrothermal reactions are abundant, including substances such as CA [82,83], ascorbic acid [84], and even strawberry juice [85]. Jia and colleagues put forth an innovative method for the facile synthesis of CDs in a single step by heating them with low-temperature water. The process involved stirring a solution of ascorbic acid and Cu(Ac)2H2O at room temperature, followed by heating to 90 °C [86]. The reaction mixture changed significantly from an orange suspension to a transparent golden solution, indicating the successful synthesis of CDs. Afterward, a substantial quantity of dispersed CDs was collected following centrifugation. Despite the fact that it requires a large amount of heat energy, hydrothermal synthesis is the most widely used method for the synthesis of CDs, because it is highly-effective, simple, and low-cost, simple to scale up, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly, offering a high production yield and easy post-treatment procedures.

Paralleling this approach, fluorescent nitrogen-enriched carbon nanoparticles (FNCPs) were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment, leveraging strawberry juice as the carbon source. Within this process, the juice was metamorphosed into nanoparticles within the extreme conditions of an autoclave, where it experienced elevated temperatures and pressures [49]. The hydrothermal treatment commenced with the transference of the juice into a Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heating at 180 °C for a duration of 12 h. The resulting products were subsequently retrieved after undergoing vacuum drying for a period of 72 h, as illustrated in Fig. 5A. The synthesized FNCP exhibited peak emission at 427 nm with a QY of 6.3 %. Notably, FNCP fluorescence could be selectively quenched by Hg2+, enabling the development of a fluorescence-based method for Hg2+ detection.

The “Bottom-up” synthesis of CDs via the solvothermal method typically involves loading the reaction substrate into a high-pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, heating it to high temperatures for the reaction, and then cooling it to yield intermediate products containing CDs [87]. Further purification and separation processes culminate in the acquisition of purified CDs. The formation of CDs from low molecular weight precursors generally involves complex molecular interactions. This transformation exemplifies the sophisticated chemistry that governs the construction of these nanoscale materials from more straightforward components. At elevated temperatures, the carbon chain skeleton undergoes continuous elongation through a cross-linking process, ultimately leading to the formation of CDs with diverse structures. This method is widely recognized in the scientific community. Moreover, CDs can be synthesized via methods like blending CA with ethylenediamine in deionized water and autoclaving, or employing ascorbic acid in a solvothermal reaction (Fig. 5B) [87]. Another method involves dispersing glutathione (GSH) in formamide and heating. Purification methods include dialysis, centrifugation, and silica gel column chromatography [93].

The solvothermal synthesis method offers excellent controllability, allowing for significant variations in product properties through the manipulation of reaction substrates, temperatures, and times. For example, using CA as the starting material and a series of six alkyl amines as the solvent medium, a one-step solvothermal synthesis of multicolored solid fluorescent CDs was achieved (Fig. 5C) [88], demonstrating how the choice of amine solvent affects the interaction between the CDs and, consequently, alters their luminescence characteristics and light emission efficiency. Meng et al. have accomplished the bulk synthesis of solid-state fluorescent carbon quantum rings with red/green/blue bandgaps (R/G/B-SBF-QRs), which exhibit a significant QY ranging from 30 % to 46 %. This advancement underscores the potential of these materials in various optoelectronic applications, particularly in the realm of high-performance warm-white-light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [89]. Using specific precursors such as p-phenyldinitrile and p-phenylenedicarboxylic acid, carbon quantum rings were synthesized by solvothermal treatment at 120–160 °C for different durations, resulting from the fusion of bent carbon quantum bands of different lengths (Fig. 5D). In an alternative approach, a mixture of acrylamide, N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide and potassium persulfate in deionized water was subjected to a one-step solvothermal polymerization and carbonization process. The mixture was heated to 200 °C and held for 8 h, then gradually cooled to room temperature [94]. The resulting products were purified through filtration and dialysis, yielding solid CDs after freeze-drying. By replacing acrylamide with acrylic acid, carboxyl-functionalized CDs could be obtained. Pyrene acted as the base material for the extensive fabrication of highly luminescent, water-soluble GQDs. Through a series of chemical treatments involving nitration, dispersion, hydrothermal reaction, filtration, and dialysis, GQDs were successfully synthesized. Additionally, the production of amino-functionalized GQDs can be achieved by utilizing varying concentrations of ammonia or hydrazine in place of a NaOH solution. This strategic substitution not only streamlines the mass production of GQDs but also ensures their excellent dispersibility in water ( Fig. 5
E and F) [90]. The critical impact of reaction temperature and time on the formation and structural development of CDs underscores the importance of selecting appropriate precursors for functionalizing CDs. Grasp of the CDs' formation mechanism is essential, as it allows for the fine-tuning of reaction parameters needed to efficiently produce the intended CDs products.

The solvothermal method is versatile, extending beyond small molecules to include biomass macromolecules. Pomelo peel, an inexpensive waste product, can serve as a precursor for creating water-dispersible fluorescent carbon nanoparticles through the hydrothermal process [91]. These particles exhibit a QY of approximately 6.9 % and have been effectively utilized for detecting Hg2+ with remarkable sensitivity and selectivity, as depicted in Fig. 5G. In a pioneering effort, Liu and colleagues effectively synthesized CDs with a high QY by employing bamboo leaves as a carbonaceous feedstock through the hydrothermal method. They created a luminescent sensor for the precise and specific identification of Cu2+ ions by encapsulating CDs with branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI) through electrostatic adsorption, as depicted in Fig. 5H [92]. The extraction of CDs from biomass mirrors a cooking process, where high temperatures and pressures break down and dissolve macromolecules into a solution, creating a “CDs soup”. This method yields naturally diverse CDs with various properties due to the range of biomass sources and abundant raw materials available. While biomass offers ample resources for large-scale CD synthesis, this approach lacks directionality, relying on iterative attempts rather than facilitating the targeted synthesis of functional CDs. Distinct among its peers, the hydrothermal process excels in the preservation of precursors' functional integrity and bioactivity, thereby earning its widespread application in the crafting of functional CDs.

The microwave technique offers a prominent and convenient strategy for the swift transformation of organic substances into CDs through the application of microwave energy. This technique offers advantages in terms of efficiency, simplicity and equipment requirements, making it a potential approach for the mass production of fluorescent CDs. For example, a cost-effective microwave procedure can produce luminescent CDs in a matter of minutes [51]. Initially, a solution comprising varying proportions of polyethylene glycol and sugar underwent heating in a 500 W microwave oven for a duration ranging from 2 to 10 min. Gradually, the solution transitioned from a transparent state to a yellow hue, eventually culminating in a deep brown coloration, indicative of the formation of CDs, as depicted in Fig. 6A. Concomitantly, a synthesis approach for the swift fabrication of solid-state fluorescent CDs exhibiting diverse emission hues through a straightforward microwave technique using the same precursor materials were delineated [52]. A mixture comprising CA, L-cysteine, and NaOH was heated in a standard household microwave oven, operating at 750 W. The heating process was conducted at varying power levels, medium and high, for a duration of around 3 min, resulting in a light-yellow solution. Upon reaching room temperature, the material was diluted with deionized water, followed by an 8 h dialysis process. The final step was freeze-drying, which resulted in the acquisition of powdered cyclodextrins, as illustrated in Fig. 6B. In the realm of biology, a novel class of red-fluorescent RNA-targeting CDs (M-CDs) has been successfully synthesized using a microwave-assisted method. This technique has been adeptly utilized to fabricate these cutting-edge CDs, which are engineered for targeted interactions with RNA molecules. The precursors chosen for this process were neutral red and levofloxacin. The synthesis entailed subjecting the precursors to microwave irradiation at 800 W for a duration of 7 min. Following this, a purification process that included both filtration and dialysis was executed, as shown in Fig. 6C. The M-CDs were successfully harvested with a yield of 22 ± 0.6 %. The one-step microwave-assisted synthesis technique is distinguished by its remarkable capacity to surmount the common challenges of traditional hydrothermal methods used in nanomaterial synthesis, such as uneven heating, extensive processing durations, and diminished product yields [95]. This approach effectively circumvents the usual challenges encountered in the conventional hydrothermal synthesis of nanomaterials, such as the problems of uneven thermal distribution and prolonged reaction times, by employing an efficient and accelerated microwave synthesis process.

The microwave technique, when integrated with other synthetic methods, forms a collective known as microwave-assisted techniques. Researchers such as Iskandar have successfully utilized this approach for the synthesis of pyrrole-N-enriched CDs. These CDs have been found to possess an absorption peak within the first near-infrared (NIR) window, showcasing their potential for advanced optical applications [53,54]. The mixture of CA and urea was first prepared in ultrapure water. This precursor solution was then transferred to a glass vial for microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment, which was carried out at various reaction times and temperatures. This method allowed for the exploration of different conditions to optimize the synthesis. The product was purified through a regenerated cellulose filter, yielding the CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 6
D and E. Alternatively, N-doped CDs can be prepared by microwave-assisted pyrolysis [96]. In this method, a uniform mixture was crafted by combining double-distilled water with urea and d-glucose. Subsequently, the solution was subjected to microwave irradiation for a duration of 15 min using a conventional household microwave oven. Once all the water had evaporated, a white precipitate was left behind. Upon further carbonization, this precipitate transformed into the final product of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs). It is important to highlight that in microwave-assisted processes, microwave energy is used to increase the temperature of the mixture to the necessary threshold, rather than directly initiating the chemical reaction on the substrate. This approach offers faster and more uniform heating compared to traditional methods, crucial for the purity of synthetic products which were subsequently carbonized to form the final N-doped CDs. Significantly, the microwave in assisted methods serves to supply the necessary thermal energy to achieve the desired temperature, instead of being the direct catalyst for the reaction occurring on the substrate. This technique provides accelerated and consistent heating surpassing that of conventional approaches, which is vital for the purity of the synthesized products. Microwave irradiation, renowned for its rapid and even heating, is increasingly preferred for its simplicity, speed, and luminescent efficacy in synthesizing CDs with diverse emission spectra. Yet, its limited penetration depth poses a scalability issue in large reactors, potentially degrading reaction performance and hindering industrial-scale production. Addressing this challenge is key to optimizing microwave synthesis for high-purity CDs on a grand scale.

The production of CDs is enhanced by the scaffolding approach, which utilizes a tailored substrate as a scaffold. The synthesis of CDs starts with the template, followed by their separation by eliminating the template, which can be achieved by acid dissolution strategies or alternative techniques. Liu et al. introduced an innovative approach by utilizing surfactant-modified silica nanospheres as a medium for the direct synthesis of amorphous CDs in an aqueous environment [98]. Specifically, they fabricated a satellite-like composite material by coating silica colloidal spheres with the amphiphilic triblock copolymer F127, resulting in the formation of a polymer/F127/silica hybrid. These functionalized spheres served as anchors for phenolic resin, serving as the carbon precursor. Following thermal processing, the silica carriers were eliminated, yielding nano-sized CDs. These CDs were subsequently rendered water-dispersible and photoluminescent through acid treatment and surface passivation. Notably, the modified silica nanospheres played a dual role: they provided attachment points for resin polymerization in solution while also preventing CD agglomeration during pyrolysis. This approach resulted in the production of over 10 % of CDs with relatively uniform particle sizes and satisfactory aqueous dispersibility, despite the complexity inherent in the synthesis procedure.

Template pyrolysis can amalgamate “Bottom-up” and “Top-down” methods for CDs synthesis. Here, templates act as scaffolds for the reaction, controlling the nucleation and growth of CDs, which results in a consistent particle size and improved solubility. Yet, the intricacies of template synthesis and subsequent extraction from the product add significant challenges to the entire manufacturing workflow. Furthermore, the recyclability of templates has a direct impact on synthesis efficiency. Consequently, while templates excel at directing CDs structure, their use is less suitable for mass-production scenarios. Template-assisted synthesis is distinguished by its precision in CDs fabrication, ensuring uniform particle size and high fluorescence quantum yield, which are conducive to subsequent surface modifications. Despite its advantages, this method faces challenges in scaling up and separating templates from CDs, potentially compromising purity and luminescent properties, thus limiting its application in mass production of CDs.

Hou and his colleagues introduced a groundbreaking method for the preparation of CDs in 2015, utilizing a straightforward blend of acetone and sodium hydroxide [55]. Remarkably, without any additional treatment, the mixture underwent spontaneous darkening within a few days due to the solidification of liquid acetone into novel products. Subsequent contemporary analytical techniques unequivocally established that these high-yield substances were indeed CDs. Typically, a mixture of sodium hydroxide and acetone was agitated for one hour under the influence of strong magnetic stirring. After 120 h under typical atmospheric conditions, the solution was then treated with diluted hydrochloric acid to adjust the pH to a neutral level. After being subjected to regular atmospheric conditions for a period of 120 h, the solution's pH was balanced by the addition of diluted hydrochloric acid. Subsequently, the product was isolated by centrifugal separation and underwent multiple rinsing cycles using purified water. Eventually, the CDs powder was harvested by the evaporation process of the final product at 100 °C for 12 h. Interestingly, additional research has discovered that the byproducts of the reaction, if left untreated, can be transformed into three-dimensional, porous carbon frameworks. These frameworks have exhibited exceptional performance when employed as anode materials in sodium-ion batteries, highlighting their enhanced battery capabilities. Crucially, this method avoids severe processing requirements like high-temperature treatments and hazardous chemicals. Furthermore, it offers a high yield with simple downstream processing, underscoring its potential for scalable production.

The alkaline conditions promote the aldol condensation of acetone or other aldehydes, leading to the formation of unsaturated ketones and aldehydes. Subsequently, polymer-like products emerge through the polymerization of these unsaturated compounds. The twisting and interconnection of these chains give rise to numerous nanodots. Under such extremely alkaline conditions, dehydration and hydroxylation reactions may occur as potential side reactions. Ultimately, the final product consists of oligomers, as confirmed previously. Furthermore, replacing acetone with acetaldehyde enabled the preparation of 3–5 nm CDs [56]. Optimizing the process improved synthesis efficiency. After further enhancement, the method can achieve kilogram yields within 2 h [40]. The aldol condensation of acetaldehyde with sodium hydroxide, followed by the addition of carbamide or cysteine, yielded N-doped and N, S-co-doped CDs [99]. To sum up, aldol condensation demonstrates great potential for large-scale CDs synthesis. The diversity of raw materials conveniently enables various heteroatom-doped CDs structures. The Aldol condensation technique, esteemed for its straightforward processing demands and robust CDs yields, confronts inherent challenges. The propensity for side reactions to disrupt the desired polymerization and small molecule reactions highlights a critical need for meticulous optimization. Such strategic refinement is essential to fully exploit the method's synthetic potential and engineer CDs with tailored properties.

The application of inductive and microwave heating techniques for the processing of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and conductive sensors has gained significant popularity. Their broad acceptance stems from the efficiency and versatility these methods offer in a range of applications, particularly those that involve the heating of MNPs and sensors [61,62,100]. This non-contact heating technology enables the absorption and conversion of electromagnetic energy on specific materials, directing heat precisely to where it is needed. This method offers a novel approach to heating, surpassing the constraints of conventional heat transfer mechanisms inherent in direct-contact heating systems. It enables direct, instantaneous energy application to the target material, significantly reducing thermal inertia. Moreover, this process is renowned for its remarkable heating efficiency, establishing it as a potent technique in thermal processing. This methodology offers a unique heating alternative, adeptly overcoming the heat transfer challenges commonly encountered in traditional contact-based heating systems. It enables the direct and immediate transfer of energy to the target sample, bypassing significant thermal barriers. Moreover, this technique is characterized by its exceptionally high heating efficiency, which sets it apart in the field of thermal processing [101]. Chen's team was the pioneer in utilizing citrate and urea as precursors for the mass production of fluorescent Zn2+, Na+, and K+-doped CDs through a rapid electromagnetic induction heating method [61].

This cutting-edge manufacturing technique fully harnessed the magnetothermal effect to synthesize CDs within a mere hour, achieving an impressive output of up to 85 g. The CDs yield was boosted by a staggering factor of approximately 160 compared to conventional methods. Superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles rapidly heated the precursor to 230 °C in 3 min, catalyzing the reaction. The oscillating magnetic moment under an alternating field induced carbonization of the precursor to form CDs. The CDs were subsequently distributed in water and subjected to an external magnetic field post-cooling.

The resulting mixture underwent magnetic separation to yield a suspension of brown CDs and a solid Fe3O4 component. Subsequent ultrafiltration led to the drying and collection of the CDs dispersion. Notably, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles can be easily recycled for a minimum of three cycles following a simple treatment. Throughout the synthesis process, the precursor undergoes initial condensation and polymerization, giving rise to interlinked polymer chains that eventually carbonize into an extensive sp2 domain. The majority of the precursors dehydrate and react to form polymer dots when the temperatures are less than 192 °C. As the reaction temperature increases, the interconnected molecules experience carbonization and graphitization, leading to the development of a characteristic CDs framework, where hydrophilic polymer chains are wrapped around the carbon nucleus. However, excessive carbonization may occur at elevated temperatures. The complex surface chemistry of the CDs produced was utilized as a nanofiller to significantly improve the mechanical properties of polycaprolactone nanofibers. These nano-fibers were crafted through the electrospinning process, playing a pivotal role in enhancing the effectiveness of wound healing. Leveraging this technique, the research team subsequently reproduced the method to produce blue-green luminescent CDs. They successfully obtained a significant yield, accomplishing the process in approximately 1.5 min [62]. Employing electromagnetic induction heating offers substantial benefits, particularly in enhancing heating performance. It notably reduces reaction times and ensures uniformity in the quality of reaction products. This method is essential for fabricating CDs with consistent morphology and size, underscoring its precision and efficacy in the synthesis process. The electromagnetic induction heating apparatus is characterized by its high initial investment, with inductive heaters predominantly effective for materials of superior electrical conductivity.

When exposed to elevated temperatures, a gas ionizes to form plasma, where positive and negative charges interact, leading to high temperatures ideal for carbonization. This property is utilized in CD production. Jiang et al. utilized helium plasma to generate free radicals, integrating ethylenediamine into the synthesis of CDs derived from benzene, yielding carbon nanoparticles with enhanced functionality. High-speed centrifugation separated functional nanoparticles, which were washed to remove impurities [102]. Acrylamide was used to synthesize photoluminescent CDs with blue fluorescence, suitable for LEDs [63]. The procedure entailed subjecting acrylamide to air plasma treatment in a glass dish for a duration of 10 min, utilizing a radiofrequency power of 150 W. Atmospheric plasma treatment has emerged as an innovative method for surface modification, aimed at augmenting the luminescent properties and bactericidal efficacy of the synthesized CDs (AC-paints) [103]. With their high fluorescence and low toxicity, AC-paints are ideal for biological imaging, producing vivid fluorescence emissions. Plasma treatment with gases such as argon, oxygen or nitrogen enhances the functionality of CDs by increasing their fluorescence and antibacterial properties.

Unlike conventional methods that necessitate high temperatures and additives, atmospheric plasma treatment stands apart as it is effortlessly administered at room temperature, eliminating the need for additives and enabling the efficient mass production of AC-paints. Employing a thermal plasma jet represents the most cost-effective manufacturing method for the production of GQDs with precise size control [64]. In this cutting-edge process, ethylene gas is continuously infused into an Ar plasma, generating a continuous stream of carbon atoms. These carbon atoms are then efficiently transported into a chamber via a carbon conduit linked to the anode. GQDs and other carbon materials are synthesized through gas-phase collision reactions, ensuring their dispersion in common solvents is avoided. By precisely manipulating the length of the carbon conduit, the dimensions of the GQDs can be accurately regulated, providing an economical, scalable production technique with exact dimensional precision. Plasma serves as an innovative reaction medium, either facilitating reactions internally or providing external energy to stimulate reactions. Essentially, adjusting the composition of gases in the plasma phase allows for simultaneous functionalization, highlighting the distinctive benefits of this innovative technique. This method capitalizes on the ability to control the plasma environment to confer specific properties to the synthesized materials. Moreover, room-temperature plasma techniques, free from the necessity for additives, present an economical and scalable production approach, ensuring precise size control and thus emerging as a sophisticated method for CDs fabrication.

The utilization of micro-reactors represents a cutting-edge tool in chemical research. Within these micro-scale reactors, synthesis and transformations occur in a lateral dimension typically measuring less than 1 mm [104]. The realm of micro-reactor-controlled synthesis has garnered substantial interest across diverse fields, encompassing organic synthesis, biology, and medicine [105,106]. By size reduction, micro-reactors exhibit a host of distinctive attributes that are unattainable through conventional batch reactors. The micro-reactor stands out for its ability to provide a spacious surface relative to its compact volume, ensuring swift movement of heat and substances, along with fast and accurate analysis. It allows for individual adjustment of various reaction elements and enhances the ability to precisely control reaction kinetics. Lin et al. pioneered the application of a microreactor synthesis system in the exploration of CDs [106]. In detail, formamide containing glucose was infused into a Teflon-lined capillary, which was then subjected to an oil bath at a temperature of 180 °C. After heating, the resulting product was collected and processed by vacuum distillation and dialysis in preparation for CDs characterization.

Using the micro-reactor, a variety of reaction conditions have been carefully curated to achieve tunable emission peaks coupled with significant photoluminescence QY (PL QY). By finely regulating the reaction duration, the facile preparation of CDs at varying developmental stages was made possible. Through the exploration of CDs at distinct reaction phases, a deep comprehension of how the PL properties of CDs correlate with the conditions of the synthesis process was revealed. This study unequivocally demonstrated that the microreactor serves not only as a potent tool for swiftly assessing the reaction dynamics of CDs but also holds paramount significance for theoretical exploration inquiry. The microfluidic system exhibits the capability to autonomously synthesize CDs, marking a significant advancement in the field [65].

Notably, the system featured a dual autonomous setup: one dedicated to thermal control and the other to microfluidics. Using vitamin C and a heat-induced breakdown process in DMSO as the medium, homogeneous and monodisperse CDs with a precise diameter of 3.3 nm were successfully created for detecting pH levels. Extensive investigations into the cytotoxicity and permeability of these CDs underscored their aptness for biomedical uses, particularly in advanced bioimaging techniques. Moreover, an innovative microfluidic approach enables the continuous production of CDs emitting fluorescence across the entire spectrum [107]. This pioneering method of producing CDs can be accomplished in just 20 min, allowing for the precise identification of Fe3+ ions and facilitating high-definition visualization in cellular studies. In a more sophisticated process, CA and urea were meticulously dissolved in an aqueous solution that was efficiently propelled through the piping system by a pair of lateral flow pumps. Following the preliminary stages, the specimen was meticulously rinsed using a blend of acetone and methanol, then subjected to lyophilization to yield the end product. The microfluidic approach, celebrated for its adept control over fluid dynamics and droplet precision, streamlines high-throughput CDs production with exceptional particle uniformity, outperforming autoclave treatments. This underscores the method's material uniformity and efficient energy transfer within microreactors. However, this technology's reliance on intricate chip design, specialized equipment, and expert handling demands high technical acumen, potentially restricting its scalability in mass production.

The burgeoning fascination with CDs stems not merely from their exceptional physicochemical attributes but also their enzyme-mimetic characteristics [1,39,108–112]. In contrast to naturally occurring enzymes, CDs, acting as nanozymes, offer the advantages of being more straightforward to synthesize, exhibiting greater longevity, and providing consistent reliability [113]. These advantageous characteristics have positioned CDs as premier contenders for a myriad of applications in sensing, biomedicine, and so on. The demonstration of their enzyme-mimicking abilities primarily revolves around pristine CDs that possess inherent enzyme-mimetic properties, the introduction of heteroatoms into CDs, and the fabrication of composite nanozymes through the integration of CDs with additional substances. As for the pristine CDs exhibiting enzyme-like functions, a biocompatible nanozyme was engineered using CDs extracted from ursolic acid (UA), a natural compound present in apples. Interestingly, these CDs exhibit strong GSH oxidase-like properties, which induced cancer cell ferroptosis by disrupting GPX4-mediated lipid repair mechanisms. In vivo, the CDs effectively inhibited tumor growth in mice with H22 tumors without adverse effects. Importantly, in mice with hepatoma H22 tumors, the CDs attracted immune cells such as T cells, NK cells, and macrophages, converting “cold” tumors into “hot” that initiate a comprehensive immune reaction against cancer (Fig. 7A). The findings of our study propose that CDs derived from products could function as benign nanozymes for cancer treatment, potentially propelling the progress of nanotechnology-driven immunotherapy [114]. Similarly, the ChA CQDs nanozyme derived from chlorogenic acid (Fig. 7B), a prominent bioactive compound found in coffee also demonstrated obvious GSH oxidase-like properties [35]. Zhu and colleagues have demonstrated that chemically oxidized β-cyclodextrin-derived CDs exhibit peroxidase-like activity [115]. Furthermore, Das and colleagues explored the peroxidase-like characteristics of pristine CDs. Findings revealed that the oxygen-containing functional groups, predominantly carboxylic acids, on the surface of CDs are the key contributors to their observed peroxidase-like activity [116]. Like their natural enzyme counterparts, the catalytic efficiency of CDs is significantly influenced by pH levels, temperature, and the concentration of hydrogen peroxide H2O2 [19,115]. Nonetheless, CDs exhibit remarkable stability across a broader spectrum of these environmental conditions Consequently, CDs, with their inherent benefits of durability and stability, emerge as a superior substitute for natural enzymes in the realms of biosensing and environmental science applications, particularly highlighting the impact of material precursors on the enzymatic-like properties of CDs.

On the flip side, the intrinsic properties of CDs are often augmented by doping with metal or non-metal atoms, a strategy that has been extensively discussed in the literature for its impact on the optical characteristics of CDs. Existing scholarly articles extensively explore the impact of doping on the photic attributes of CDs [121]. For example, Cu-doped CDs engineered by Duan and colleagues exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like activity, surpassing that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and displaying robustness over a wider range of pH and temperature conditions. The improved catalytic efficiency was ascribed to the electronic feature achieved through the incorporation copper (Fig. 7C) [117]. A multifunctional copper-based composite, denoted as FG-CDs@Cu was effectively synthesized for the specific targeting of CT26 mouse colon cancer cells. The incorporation of copper ions conferred upon it GSH oxidase-like activity, ultimately leading to the induction of ferroptosis and the stimulation of the tumor microenvironment (TME) for immunotherapeutic purposes (Fig. 7D) [118]. The research team also reported copper nanocrystals doped with folic acid (FA)--based super CDs (FA-CDs@Cux) to enhance the anti-tumor efficacy in TME. Due to the doping of copper ions, the FA-CDs@Cux exhibited the capability to diminish intracellular H2O2 to ⋅OH and effectively deplete GSH within tumor cells, thus demonstrating a pronounced enzyme-like activity. Furthermore, the incorporation of copper metal facilitated the capacity to absorb near-infrared laser, boasting a remarkable photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 54.3 %. Additionally, it achieves a singlet oxygen (1O2) atom yield of 56.83 %, endowing the super FA-CDs@Cux with synergistic function encompassing chemodynamic therapy (CDT)/PTT/PDT under TME and NIR stimulation (Fig. 7E) [34]. The peroxidase-like behavior demonstrated by N-CDs and iron-doped CDs has opened up new avenues for the development of a highly sensitive colorimetric method for the specific detection of H2O2 and glucose [122]. Therefore, the incorporation of various elements acts as a potent method to finely tune the surface chemical reactivity of CDs, thereby enhancing their physicochemical properties.

CDs can be synthesized from a variety of precursors, including citric acid, glucose, β-cyclodextrin, l-ascorbic acid, graphene oxide, graphite, and coal. The choice of precursor is pivotal to the enzymatic-like activity of CDs, as it significantly influences their properties. The impact of precursors on CDs' properties is linked to the formation of distinct structures, such as varying conjugation domains and levels of graphitization within the carbon core, different functional groups on the surface, and varying degrees of functionalization that occur during the carbonization of the precursors [123]. A substantial body of research has concentrated on how precursors affect the optical properties of CDs, which are crucial for their enzyme-mimicking activities [121,124,125]. For instance, Mozdbar et al. synthesized CDs using citric acid and three nitrogen-containing precursors-thiourea, ethylamine, and monoethanolamine-and found that those derived from citric acid and monoethanolamine had a broader absorption band, higher production efficiency, and greater emission quantum yield compared to CDs from other precursors [126]. This suggests that the precursors play a critical role in determining the CDs' ability to mimic enzymatic activity through their optical properties. Similarly, Hasan et al. extracted CDs from three distinct biomass precursors, observing variations in average diameter size, surface groups, and emission capacities, which are all factors that can influence the enzymatic-like behavior of CDs [127]. Therefore, the selection of precursor is essential in tailoring the enzyme-like activity of CDs for various applications.

The influence of precursors on the enzymatic activity of carbon materials has been largely overlooked. To decipher the peroxidase-mimicking mechanism of CDs, Das et al. conducted a study on the impact of four distinct compounds-acetic acid, acetone, ethanol, and aniline-each characterized by a unique functional group: carboxylic, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups, respectively [116]. Their findings revealed that acetic acid was the most effective in conferring peroxidase-like activity, while ethanol and aniline were less conducive to the reaction. This implies that the presence of carboxylic groups is essential for enhancing peroxidase-like activity in CDs. Extending their investigation, they utilized various carboxylic acid-containing compounds as precursors for CD synthesis. Notably, CDs derived from L-glutamic acid exhibited a significantly higher peroxidase-like activity compared to other precursors, significantly outperforming CDs from glycine, l-glutamine, and acetic acid, which lack a sufficient number of carboxylic groups [116]. In a related context, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) sourced from different types of coal-anthracite and bituminous-have shown variations in their superoxide dismutase-like activity, indicating that the choice of starting material significantly affects their enzyme-mimicking capabilities [128]. Thus, the judicious selection of precursors can augment not only the optical properties but also the enzymatic activity of carbon materials.

Researchers have also explored the synthesis of hybrid nanozymes, in which CDs are fused with different materials to increase their catalytic efficiency through a cooperative effect. For example, Ngo and colleagues created N-CDs/manganese oxide/iron oxide composites that have inherent peroxidase-like activity. The results unveiled that these hybrid nanoparticles exhibited exceptional catalytic performance compared to pristine N-doped CDs, emphasizing the synergistic synergy between CDs and metal oxides (Fig. 7F) [129]. Nevertheless, the sophisticated development of hybrid nanozymes may come with certain limitations, including the need for elaborate and labor-intensive procedures and the frequent use of reducing agents [120]. Noteworthy research studies concentrating on CDs possessing enzyme-mimetic characteristics achieved through various methods were succinctly summarized in Table 2 .

The impact of precursors on the enzyme-mimicking capabilities of CDs has received relatively scant attention compared to their optical properties. In a bid to unravel the underlying mechanisms of peroxidase-mimicking CDs. In a study conducted by Das and collaborators, the influence of four distinct compounds - acetic acid, acetone, ethanol, and aniline - each characterized by a unique functional group was thoroughly examined. Their results showed that acetic acid exhibited superior peroxidase-like activity compared to other substances, while ethanol and aniline were less effective, highlighting the significant influence of carboxylic groups on peroxidase-like activity. Subsequently, a variety of carboxylic acid-containing compounds were used as precursors for the synthesis of CDs. CDs synthesized from L-glutamic acid exhibited a remarkable peroxidase-like activity, surpassing the performance of CDs derived from glycine, l-glutamine, and acetic acid due to the higher concentration of carboxylic groups present [116]. Similarly, GQDs sourced from different coal reservoirs exhibited variations in their SOD-like activity, underscoring the influence of the source material on their enzyme-mimicking capacities [128].This underscores the significance of judiciously selecting precursors not only to enhance optical properties but also to augment enzyme-mimicking functionalities.

Peroxidases catalyze oxidative transformations in the presence of peroxides, yielding water as a byproduct [18,134–136]. The discovery of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) reveals their innate peroxidase-like capabilities, effectively emulating the catalytic efficacy of HRP on substrates such as TMB, DAB, and OPD (Fig. 8A) [18]. The catalytic prowess of these MNPs is contingent upon factors such as pH, temperature, H2O2 concentration, and particle dimensions. Fe3O4 MNPs demonstrate a broad pH tolerance for peroxidase activity, superior thermal stability, and expedited reaction kinetics when compared to HRP. They function through a “ping-pong” mechanism, engaging with H2O2 and mediating electron transfer from substrates to generate oxidized products. The coexistence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions endowed these nanozymes with the resilience to sustain catalytic activity amidst an excess of H2O2 or other oxidizing agents, positioning them as robust surrogates to traditional peroxidases [137].

The catalytic mechanism of peroxidase involves the oxidation of the enzyme's active site, typically a heme group, which then transfers an oxygen atom to the substrate. In natural peroxidases, a heme group is typically present within their structure, playing a key role in catalytic reactions. The isoform carbon of HRP is a well-studied peroxidase that features an iron center with two vacant coordination positions. The catalytic cycle of this enzyme involves three oxidation stages of iron, starting with the binding of peroxide to the heme group to form an unstable peroxide complex, followed by the generation of Compound I and Compound II through oxidation and reduction processes, ultimately regenerating the enzyme (Fig. 8B) [138].

Additionally, nanoparticle-based peroxidases often undergo Fenton reactions, where the nanomaterial directly reduces H2O2 to ⋅OH, although the specific mechanism can vary based on the nanoparticle composition [116]. In the quest to mimic the enzyme HRP, a key strategy has been to enrich CDs with hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxylic, carbonyl, hydroxyl and amine groups. Recent studies suggest that the peroxidase-like activity observed in certain materials is predominantly a surface-mediated event due to hydrogen bonding-assisted interactions involving carboxylic acid moieties and hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 8C) [116]. Specifically, the interaction of CDs surface functional groups with H2O2 is pivotal in activating the peroxide and facilitating the transfer of an oxygen atom to the substrate. This mechanism mirrors the “ping-pong” mechanism characteristic of natural peroxidases, wherein a reactive intermediate is generated upon the binding of H2O2 to the enzyme's active site. Furthermore, the conjugated sp2 domains on the CDs' surface are instrumental in electron transfer, with the delocalization of electrons enabling the reduction of H2O2 and the subsequent oxidation of substrates, such as TMB and ABTS. Additionally, the catalytic activity of CDs as peroxidase mimics is subject to modulation by pH and temperature, akin to natural enzymes. Notably, acidic conditions tend to augment the catalytic efficiency of CDs, potentially owing to the protonation of surface functional groups, which in turn, enhances the electron transfer process [137].

A novel approach involves mimicking the amino acid microenvironment within the material, exemplified by the development of GQDs incorporating the heme group, as showcased by the histidine-functionalized GQDs (His-GQD)/hemin complex unveiled in 2020 [139]. Elaborate analyses have unveiled a “ping-pong” or an alternative catalytic mechanism that has been identified for this mimetic system, deviating from the traditional sequential process. The catalytic performance of CDs as enzyme mimics, particularly in their peroxidase-like activity, has been extensively studied. The influence of CDs' properties on their catalytic efficiency remains a key focus of investigation. The assessment of the inherent peroxidase-like capability of CDs commonly entails catalyzing the oxidation of chromogenic substrates such as TMB and ABTS in the presence of H2O2. The production of ⋅OH during this oxidation process is essential, as nanozymes, including those based on CDs, possess the ability to break down H2O2 into ⋅OH, which is vital for their peroxidase-mimetic functions [140]. Studies have shown that CDs-based nanozymes exhibited increased ⋅OH generation during the oxidative reactions involving TMB and ABTS, which is due to the nanoscale dimensions and expansive surface area of CDs that promote the adsorption of H2O2 and its subsequent dissociation into ⋅OH. Additionally, electron transfer mechanisms are crucial in these catalytic processes. The augmented electron transfer between the amine group of TMB and CDs has been associated with the innate peroxidase-like activity of CDs, leading to increased surface electron density and accelerated H2O2 decomposition [140,141].

The dual role of CDs in facilitating electron exchange between TMB and H2O2, together with their ability to enhance radical generation, highlights their peroxidase-mimicking ability. Various studies have highlighted that pristine CDs or doped-CDs, such as N, and Fe-CDs, can effectively mediate the oxidative reactions of peroxidase substrates, exhibiting catalytic activity that is comparable or even superior to that of the natural enzyme HRP (Fig. 8D) [142]. For example, N, Fe-CDs showed a higher affinity towards TMB and H2O2 compared to HRP, as indicated by lower Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) values. The kinetic parameters of various CDs-based nanozymes suggest that lower Km values indicate higher substrate affinity, while higher Vmax values indicate greater catalytic activity [122,129]. In addition, CDs-derived have shown exhibited enhanced peroxidase-mimicking activities, outperforming both the natural enzyme HRP and several other nanomaterials. For example, a GQDs/CuO hybrid nanozyme exhibited a significantly lower Km value for H2O2 as a substrate compared to copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs), highlighting the effectiveness of CDs-based nanozymes in catalytic reactions [143].

Biocatalysts typically display high efficiency in benign reaction settings, yet their robustness is challenged in severe conditions. For example, HRP's enzymatic function is notably reduced above 30 °C [145], and its effectiveness is constrained within a narrow pH and concentration range [18]. In addition, the catalytic potency of CDs is limited to certain pH values and concentrations. As with other biocatalysts, parameters such as pH, temperature and substrate concentration significantly affect the catalytic efficiency of CDs (Table 3 ). However, CDs demonstrate a broader tolerance to varying pH, temperature, and concentration conditions [133,146]. For instance, Wu et al. conducted a study on a GQDs/Fe3O4 hybrid nanozyme, revealing that its catalytic activity remained stable within the temperature range of 20 to 80 °C, with optimal activity observed at 50 °C [133]. Comparable findings have been reported by other researchers [145–147].

In terms of substrate concentration, Zhu et al. observed that elevated H2O2 levels could suppress the peroxidase-mimicking activity of CDs. Nevertheless, compared to HRP, CDs nanozymes exhibited enhanced performance across a wider concentration spectrum [146]. Similarly, Tang et al. investigated the influence of TMB concentration on the peroxidase-mimicking behavior of CDs and observed a peak in absorbance followed by a decline at TMB concentrations of 0.20 mM [130]. The effective operational range for nanozymes based on CDs typically spans from 0.2 to 0.8 mM [122,130]; However, the latest research underscores that the peak peroxidase-like efficiency for the MnxOy/Fe2O3/N-CDs composite is observed at a TMB concentration of 10 mM, indicating the importance of nanozyme-specific catalytic conditions for achieving the best performance [129].

Moreover, the concentration of the catalyst plays a crucial role in catalytic activity. One study indicated that pristine CDs reached maximum peroxidase-like activity at an optimal concentration of 10 μg/mL [146]. Conversely, doped CDs such as N, Fe-CDs synthesized by Li et al., have demonstrated enhanced peroxidase-like properties at higher concentrations, requiring an optimal dosage of 150 μg/mL for peak performance [122]. The influence of pH on the peroxidase-like activity exhibited by CDs has been extensively investigated in prior studies [122,129,130]. Overall, these studies concur that CDs-based nanozymes demonstrate maximum peroxidase-like activity under acidic conditions, primarily due to the acceleration of the catalytic reaction with an increase in H+ concentration and the enhanced solubility of the TMB substrate in acidic environments [140,146]. Moreover, regarding recyclability, CDs exhibiting peroxidase-like functions have exhibited enhanced efficacy in contrast to HRP. Zhu et al. conducted studies illustrating that a GQDs/Fe3O4 hybrid nanozyme preserved 64 % of its initial catalytic efficiency, surpassing the stability of HRP, which experienced a decline of over 60 % under similar conditions [146].

The current emphasis in utilizing CDs with peroxidase-like characteristics lies in their application within detection platforms for a variety of substances. An illustrative instance is the research conducted by Duan et al., where they introduced a method employing doped-CDs that demonstrated an exceptional sensitivity in detecting glucose, with a remarkable detection threshold as low as 0.32 μM [117]. Several other glucose detection systems have emerged, characterized by their robustness, simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity, leveraging the peroxidase-mimetic capabilities of CDs [117,122,129,140,148]. Similarly, Yang et al. introduced a selective dual-emission fluorescence sensing approach for the detection of H2O2 and cholesterol using doped CDs [149]. Prominent compounds detected by peroxidase-simulating CDs include GSH [142], and uric acid [120], as well as oxidizing ions such as Fe3+ and Ag+ [146].
